key_insight: A physics-informed machine-learning model shows that moderate Ti, Ni, and Co additions can improve room-temperature ductility in tungsten-based refractory high-entropy alloys, while high Cr content tends to make them brittle.
engineering_relevance: The resulting design rules can help screen tungsten alloys for fusion-relevant components by prioritizing compositions with better ductility before costly experiments.
method: Curated sparse experimental dataset of tungsten-containing alloys
Built composition-based, physics-informed descriptors and compared three classifiers with nested cross-validation
Used SHAP and synthetic compositions on pseudo-ternary diagrams to interpret and map ductility trends
evidence_signal: A support vector classifier gave the best generalization in nested cross-validation, and its predictions aligned with reported experimental trends such as Ti-assisted ductility and Cr-driven embrittlement.
watch_item: The dataset is small and heterogeneous, so the learned boundaries should be treated as screening guidance rather than definitive alloy-performance rules.
paper_title: Ductility Design Rules for Tungsten based Refractory High Entropy Alloys from Sparse Experimental Datasets
